note:
γ batch input. 100 clusters.

For each cluster, output a verdict in strict JSON:
  {"cluster_hash": "<hash>", "word": "<word>", "decision": "merge|split|unsure", "confidence": 0.0-1.0, "reasoning": "1 sentence", "examples_substitutable_pct": 0-100, "merged_text": "..." | null}

Save as: data/simplify_diff/gamma_batch_verdicts.json

GAMMA_SYSTEM_PROMPT:
You are an English vocabulary teacher reviewing 2-3 senses of a single word for an IELTS learner's flashcard.

YOUR RULE (the user's "80-90% rule"):
Merge the senses ONLY IF a single definition lets the learner understand AT LEAST 80-90% of all examples across the senses. Otherwise SPLIT.

OUTPUT FORMAT (strict JSON, no prose):
{
  "decision": "merge" | "split" | "unsure",
  "confidence": 0.0-1.0,
  "reasoning": "1 sentence",
  "examples_substitutable_pct": 0-100,
  "merged_text": "single definition" | null
}

DECISION RULES:
- merge:    1 def covers >=80% examples, learner doesn't need separate cards
- split:    senses are too distinct, need 2+ cards
- unsure:   borderline 70-80%, default to SPLIT in production (conservative)

NEVER merge senses that are clearly different POS, register, or context (e.g. "go" verb vs "go" noun).

senses.text: the group of people who are elected to make and change the laws of a country
examples: The German parliament is called the ‘Bundestag’.
The Scottish parliament is expected to vote in favour of a ban.
The exit polls predicted a hung parliament (= in which no political party has more elected members than all the other parties added together).

senses.text: the parliament of some countries, including the United Kingdom. In the UK, Parliament consists of the House of Commons and the House of Lords
examples: a Member of Parliament
an Act of Parliament
The issue was debated in Parliament.
to win a seat in Parliament
The party is set to win a majority in Parliament (= the most votes) in October's election.

senses.text: the place where two or more roads or railway lines meet
examples: It was near the junction of City Road and Old Street.
Come off the motorway at junction 6.
The most complicated motorway interchange in Britain is known as Spaghetti Junction.
In the 19th century the town became an important railway junction.

senses.text: a place where two or more cables, rivers or other things meet or are joined
examples: a telephone junction box
the junction between nerves and muscles

senses.text: a state of not being certain about what is happening, what you should do, what something means, etc.
examples: The announcement caused a lot of confusion.
There is some confusion about what the correct procedure should be.
There was a confusion as to what to do next.

senses.text: the fact of making a mistake about who somebody is or what something is
examples: To avoid confusion, please write the children's names clearly on all their school clothes.
confusion between letters of the alphabet like ‘o’ or ‘a’

senses.text: a situation in which you feel embarrassed because you do not understand something and are not sure what to do
examples: He looked at me in confusion and did not answer the question.
Sacha felt confusion sweeping over her as she read the letter.

senses.text: a confused situation in which people do not know what action to take
examples: Fighting had broken out and all was chaos and confusion.
Her unexpected arrival threw us into total confusion.

senses.text: to move from one place to another; to move something/somebody from one place to another
examples: The film studio is transferring to Hollywood.
If I spend a semester in Madrid, will my credits transfer?
The system only allows air to go one way, preventing air from transferring from one tire to another.
How can I transfer money from my bank account to his?
The patient was transferred to another hospital.

senses.text: to move from one job, school, situation, etc. to another; to arrange for somebody to move
examples: Children usually transfer to secondary school at 11 or 12.
This is her first season in the Ohio State team after transferring from Tennessee.
Students can transfer from a community college to a university.
He transferred to UCLA after his freshman year.
Ten employees are being transferred from the sales department.

senses.text: to change to a different vehicle during a journey; to arrange for somebody to change to a different vehicle during a journey
examples: It was a relief, after transferring from ship to ship, to just sit and relax.
I transferred at Bahrain for a flight to Singapore.
Passengers are transferred from the airport to the hotel by taxi.

senses.text: to copy information, music, an idea, etc. from one method of recording or presenting it to another; to be recorded or presented in a different way
examples: You can transfer data to a memory stick in a few seconds.
Data is easily transferred electronically.
You can transfer songs from your computer to a phone.
The novel does not transfer well to the movies.
An audio file should transfer in a matter of minutes.

senses.text: if you transfer a feeling, a disease, or power, etc. from one person to another, the second person has it, often instead of the first
examples: Joe had already transferred his affections from Lisa to Cleo.
I decided to transfer my loyalty to my local team.
This disease is sometimes transferred from mother to baby (= so that the baby has it as well as the mother).

senses.text: a statement that something is not true or does not exist; the action of denying something
examples: the prisoner’s repeated denials of the charges against him
The terrorists issued a denial of responsibility for the attack.
There was an official denial that there would be an election before the end of the year.
She shook her head in denial.

senses.text: an act of refusing to allow somebody to have something they have a right to expect
examples: Pressure groups have drawn attention to the denial of human rights in some areas.
The advertising ban is a denial of freedom of speech.

senses.text: the act of refusing to accept that something unpleasant or painful is true
examples: The patient is still in denial.
Some people are in denial about the situation.

senses.text: connected with God or a god; considered to be holy
examples: a sacred image/shrine/temple
sacred music
Cows are sacred to Hindus.

senses.text: very important and treated with great respect; that must not be changed or challenged
examples: Human life must always be sacred.
For journalists nothing is sacred (= they write about anything).
Some companies offer five-year plans but there is nothing sacred about this length of time (= it can be changed).

senses.text: involving a lot of work or activity done in a short time
examples: an intensive language course
two weeks of intensive training
intensive diplomatic negotiations
highly intensive courses for business and professional people

senses.text: complete and extremely detailed; done with a lot of care
examples: His disappearance has been the subject of intensive investigation.

senses.text: aimed at producing as much food as possible using as little land or as little money as possible
examples: Traditionally reared animals grow more slowly than those reared under intensive farming conditions.
intensive agriculture

senses.text: a room or hall with equipment for doing physical exercise, for example in a school
examples: The school has recently built a new gym.
to play basketball in the gym

senses.text: a private club where people go to do physical exercise in order to stay or become healthy and fit
examples: I just joined a gym.
I work out at the gym most days.

senses.text: the situation when a factory, school, hospital, etc. shuts permanently
examples: factory closures
The hospital has been threatened with closure.
Spending cuts could mean the closure of the village school.

senses.text: the temporary closing of a road or bridge
examples: There will be road closures and diversions in the area from 8 p.m.
The accident caused the complete closure of the road.

senses.text: a group of words that have a particular meaning when used together
examples: a memorable/catchy phrase
‘Start slowly’ is the key phrase for the first-time marathon runner.
She was, in her own favourite phrase, ‘a woman without a past’.
colourful words and phrases

senses.text: a small group of words (usually without a finite verb) that together have a particular meaning and that typically form part of a sentence. ‘the green car’ and ‘on Friday morning’ are phrases.
examples: a two-word phrase

senses.text: a new room or rooms that are added to a house
examples: They're building an extension to their house.

senses.text: a new part that is added to a building
examples: a planned two-storey extension to the hospital

senses.text: a phone line with its own number, connected to a switchboard in a large building
examples: What's your extension number?
Can I have extension 4332 please?
I'll give you my extension number, so you can phone me directly.

senses.text: the grey or black powder that is left after something, especially tobacco, wood or coal, has burnt
examples: cigarette ash
black volcanic ash
Ash from the volcano fell over a wide area.
She flicked ash out of the window carelessly.

senses.text: what is left after something has been destroyed by burning
examples: The town was reduced to ashes in the fighting.
the glowing ashes of the campfire
The fire had died to cold ashes.
The party had risen, like a phoenix, from the ashes of electoral disaster.
She had seen all her dreams turn to ashes.

senses.text: if somebody/something has a particular tendency, they are likely to behave or act in a particular way
examples: to display artistic tendencies
I have a tendency to talk too much when I'm nervous.
This material has a tendency to shrink when washed.
There is a tendency for this disease to run in families.
She has a strong natural tendency towards caution.

senses.text: a new custom that is starting to develop
examples: There's a growing tendency for women to marry later.
Industry showed a tendency towards increasingly centralized administration.

senses.text: a law or a set of laws passed by a parliament
examples: an important piece of legislation
New legislation on the sale and use of fireworks is being considered.
They are calling for tough legislation to tackle this problem.

senses.text: the process of making and passing laws
examples: Legislation will be difficult and will take time.
The terrorist attack prompted knee-jerk legislation.

senses.text: confident and free to do things without needing help from other people
examples: Going away to college has made me much more independent.
She's a very independent-minded young woman.
Pat has always been fiercely independent.
Students should aim to become more independent of their teachers.

senses.text: having or earning enough money so that you do not have to rely on somebody else for help
examples: It was important to me to be financially independent of my parents.
a man of independent means (= with an income that he does not earn by working)

senses.text: to tell somebody about something in a way that makes it easy to understand
examples: He was trying to explain the difference between sarcasm and irony.
First, I'll explain the rules of the game.
The theory is not explained in detail.
‘Let me explain!’ he added helpfully.
It was difficult to explain the concept to beginners.

senses.text: to give a reason, or be a reason, for something
examples: I tried to explain but they wouldn't listen.
Alex explained that his car had broken down.
Well, that doesn't explain why you didn't phone.
Please explain your reasons.
Oh well then, that explains it (= I understand now why something happened).

senses.text: to watch or to look at somebody/something carefully in order to find out something
examples: Scientists are studying photographs of the planet for signs of life.
He studied her face thoughtfully.
Fran was studying the menu.
She picked up the letter and studied it carefully.

senses.text: to examine something carefully in order to understand it
examples: We will study the report carefully before making a decision.
The group will study how the region coped with the loss of thousands of jobs.
They are studying what happens to people who unexpectedly win a lot of money.

senses.text: a medical examination to discover what is wrong with you or to check the condition of your health
examples: The hospital is doing some tests.
We'll need to perform a series of tests.
screening tests for cancer
Three athletes were sent home after failing drugs tests.
The pregnancy test was positive.

senses.text: an experiment to discover whether or how well something works, or to find out more information about it
examples: the results of laboratory tests
to conduct a nuclear test
Tests have shown high levels of pollutants in the water.
I'll run a diagnostic test to determine why the server keeps crashing.

senses.text: a written or spoken account of an event, especially one that is published or broadcast
examples: Are these news reports true?
media/press/newspaper reports
And now over to Jim Muir, for a report on the South African election.
A local news station aired a special report on the controversy.
According to this evening's weather report, there will be snow tomorrow.

senses.text: a spoken or written description of something containing information that somebody needs to have
examples: a police report
The company has just released its annual report.
Can you give us a progress report?
You need to compile a report on your findings.

senses.text: an official document written by a group of people who have examined a particular situation or problem
examples: to release/issue a report
The committee will publish its report on the health service next week.
According to the report, we are facing an obesity crisis.

senses.text: the parts of something that are left after the other parts have been used, eaten, removed, etc.
examples: She fed the remains of her lunch to the dog.

senses.text: the parts of ancient objects and buildings that have survived and are discovered in the present day
examples: The museum has an impressive collection of prehistoric remains.
the remains of a Roman fort

senses.text: the body of a dead person or animal
examples: They had discovered human remains.

senses.text: the part of your mind that tells you whether your actions are right or wrong
examples: to have a clear/guilty conscience (= to feel that you have done right/wrong)
This is a matter of individual conscience (= everyone must make their own judgement about it).
He won't let it trouble his conscience.

senses.text: a guilty feeling about something you have done or failed to do
examples: She was seized by a sudden pang of conscience.
I have a terrible conscience about it.

senses.text: the fact of behaving in a way that you feel is right even though this may cause problems
examples: freedom of conscience (= the freedom to do what you believe to be right)
Emilia is the voice of conscience in the play.

senses.text: strong emotion
examples: Feeling runs deep (= people feel strongly) on this issue.
the depth/strength of feeling about an issue
She spoke with feeling about the plight of homeless people.
The debate aroused strong feelings on both sides.
Feelings are running high (= people are very angry or excited).

senses.text: an attitude or opinion about something
examples: The general feeling was against the decision.
My own feeling is that we should buy the cheaper one.
Public feeling is being ignored by the government.
I don't have any strong feelings about it one way or the other.
She had mixed feelings about giving up her job.

senses.text: a university teacher of the highest rank
examples: Professor (Ann) Williams
to be appointed professor of economics at Cambridge
He was made (a) professor at the age of 40.

senses.text: a teacher at a university or college
examples: a university/college professor
a chemistry/law professor
a professor of psychology/political science
She was my professor when I was a grad student.

senses.text: very correct and suitable for official or important occasions
examples: ladies in formal evening wear
The dinner was a formal affair.
He kept the tone of the letter formal and businesslike.
His manner was stiffly formal.

senses.text: official; following an agreed or official way of doing things
examples: formal legal processes
to make a formal complaint
There has been no formal announcement of her resignation yet.
There followed a formal request for military aid.
Formal diplomatic relations between the two countries were re-established in December.

senses.text: land; the hard surface of the world that is not the sea or the sky; the ground
examples: After a week at sea, it was good to feel the earth beneath our feet again.
fifty feet above the earth
in mines deep under the earth
You could feel the earth shake as the truck came closer.

senses.text: the substance that plants grow in
examples: a clod/mound of earth
I cleaned off the earth clinging to my boots.

senses.text: very great; very strong
examples: We were all suffering in the intense heat.
intense cold/pain
They watched with intense interest.
intense pleasure/desire/anger
The President is under intense pressure to resign.

senses.text: serious and often involving a lot of action in a short period of time
examples: There is intense competition for the top jobs.
It was a period of intense activity.

senses.text: done with speed; taking or lasting a short time
examples: a quick look/check/search
She gave him a quick glance.
Let's take a quick break.
There's no quick fix for this problem.
Just a quick note to say thanks for lunch yesterday.

senses.text: moving or doing something fast
examples: a quick learner
The kids were quick to learn.
She was quick (= too quick) to point out the mistakes I'd made.
Don't be too quick to dismiss science fiction.
Don't be so quick to judge unless you know all the facts.

senses.text: happening very soon or without delay
examples: We need to make a quick decision.
The company wants quick results.
The doctor said she'd make a quick recovery.

senses.text: to bring, give, put or send something back to somebody/something
examples: We had to return the hairdryer to the store because it was faulty.
I must return some books to the library.
The court ordered him to return the children to their mother.
Completed questionnaires should be returned to this address.
He refused to return our money.

senses.text: to come back again
examples: The following day the pain returned.
There's a chance the cancer will return.
Confidence returned and prices started to rise.
The bad weather has returned with a vengeance.

senses.text: to start discussing a subject you were discussing earlier, or doing an activity you were doing earlier
examples: He returns to this topic later in the report.
She looked up briefly then returned to her sewing.
The doctor may allow her to return to work next week.

senses.text: to go back to a previous state
examples: Train services have returned to normal after the strike.
The land has returned to its natural state.
Many find returning to civilian life difficult.

senses.text: houses, flats, etc. that people live in, especially when referring to their type, price or condition
examples: rental/student housing
temporary/permanent housing
low-cost/cheap housing
There is an urgent need to build more affordable housing.
We must find a way to solve the city's housing crisis.

senses.text: the job of providing houses, flats, etc. for people to live in
examples: Tenants may only keep pets with the written consent of the housing department.
They discussed how the council's housing policy could be changed to deal with the growing housing crisis.

senses.text: a specialist who treats a particular type of illness or problem, or who uses a particular type of treatment
examples: a speech therapist
a beauty therapist

senses.text: a person who is trained to treat somebody who has problems with their mental health by talking with them
the state of being alone and not watched or interrupted by other people
examples: She was longing for some peace and privacy.
I value my privacy.
The others left the room to give them some privacy.
He read the letter later in the privacy of his own room.

senses.text: the state of being free from the attention of the public
examples: freedom of speech and the right to privacy
privacy rights/issues
She complained that the photographs were an invasion of her privacy.
These policies are designed to protect the privacy of your personal information.
We need to have access to health records while safeguarding patient privacy.

senses.text: a building in which collections of books, newspapers, etc. and sometimes films and recorded music are kept for people to read, study or borrow
examples: a public/university/school library
a library book
a toy library (= for borrowing toys from)
How often do you go to the library?
You can borrow it from your local library.

senses.text: a room in a large house where most of the books are kept
work, especially when it is done to earn money; the state of being employed
examples: full-time/part-time employment
people seeking employment
They are finding it more and more difficult to find employment.
The steelworks provided employment for thousands of people.
He took up employment with the company in May 2015.

senses.text: the situation in which people have work
examples: The government is aiming at full employment.
Changes in farming methods have badly affected employment in the area.

senses.text: an official visit to a school, factory, etc. in order to check that rules are being obeyed and that standards are acceptable
examples: Regular inspections are carried out at the prison.
The head went on a tour of inspection of all the classrooms.

senses.text: the act of looking closely at something/somebody, especially to check that everything is as it should be
examples: The documents are available for inspection.
Engineers carried out a thorough inspection of the track.
Following an 8 a.m. pitch inspection by the referee, today's game has been called off due to a waterlogged pitch.
On closer inspection, the notes proved to be forgeries.

senses.text: to make musical sounds with your voice in the form of a song or tune
examples: I just can't sing in tune!
I always wanted to be on stage, singing and dancing.
He was dancing around and singing at the top of his voice (= very loudly).
The role is traditionally sung by a tenor.
She usually sings in the shower.

senses.text: to make high musical sounds
examples: The birds were singing outside my window.
Birds started singing because they thought dawn had broken.
Every bird in the field was singing its heart out (= singing loudly, as if it was very happy).

senses.text: an offence against God or against a religious or moral law
examples: to commit a sin
Confess your sins to God and he will forgive you.
God forgives our sins.
The Bible says that stealing is a sin.

senses.text: the act of breaking a religious or moral law
examples: a life of sin
Believers are called on to turn away from sin and embrace a life of prayer.

senses.text: a story created using the imagination, especially one that is full of action and adventure
examples: a coming-of-age/morality tale
The story is a classic tale of love and betrayal.
Dickens’ ‘A Tale of Two Cities’
His latest book is a delightful children’s tale about talking animals.

senses.text: an exciting spoken description of an event, which may not be completely true
examples: I love listening to his tales of life at sea.
I've heard tales of people seeing ghosts in that house.
She regaled us with tales of her wild youth.
The team's tale of woe continued on Saturday (= they lost another match).
Her experiences provide a cautionary tale (= a warning) for us all.

senses.text: an evil spirit
examples: The people believed the girl was possessed by demons.

senses.text: something that causes a person to worry and makes them unhappy
examples: the demons of jealousy
She's had to battle her personal demons throughout her adult life.
He is plagued by demons which go back to his traumatic childhood.

senses.text: a natural quality that makes people and animals tend to behave in a particular way using the knowledge and abilities that they were born with rather than thought or training
examples: She did not seem to have any of the usual maternal instincts.
Children do not know by instinct the difference between right and wrong.
His first instinct was to run away.
Horses have a well-developed instinct for fear.
Even at school, he showed he had an instinct for (= was naturally good at) business.

senses.text: a feeling that makes you do something or believe that something is true, even though it is not based on facts or reason
examples: I've always trusted my instincts in the past.
It's best to follow your first instincts in matters like this.
Her instincts about him had been right.
He had a gut instinct for when people were lying to him.
I acted purely on instinct.

senses.text: something that you wear over your face to hide it, or to frighten or entertain other people
examples: The robbers wore stocking masks.
The kids were all wearing animal masks.
In the second part of the play, the actors take off their masks.

senses.text: something that you wear over part or all of your face in order to protect it
examples: a fencing mask

senses.text: an act or the process of finding somebody/something, or learning about something that was not known about before
examples: Researchers in this field have made some important new discoveries.
New scientific discoveries are being made all the time.
the discovery of antibiotics in the twentieth century
The discovery of a child's body in the river has shocked the community.
the discovery of new talent in the art world

senses.text: a thing, fact or person that is found or learned about for the first time
examples: The drug is not a new discovery—it's been known about for years.
one of the most important scientific discoveries of all time
potentially the biggest archaeological discovery in Norway for fifty years
recent discoveries about sleep

senses.text: to put something somewhere and keep it there to use later
examples: We do not have adequate space to store these documents.
animals storing up food for the winter
He hoped the electronic equipment was safely stored away.
You can store coffee beans in the freezer to keep them fresh.
Specimens are stored at very low temperatures.

senses.text: to keep information or facts in a computer or in your brain
examples: to store data/information
Digital music files can be stored on your computer.
Thousands of pieces of data are stored in a computer's memory.

senses.text: the process of preparing food by heating it
examples: My husband does all the cooking.
a book on Indian cooking
We always use olive oil for cooking.
Cooking is her passion.

senses.text: food that has been prepared in a particular way
examples: The restaurant offers traditional home cooking (= food similar to that cooked at home).
They serve good French cooking.

senses.text: to make things to be sold, especially in large quantities
examples: Our company mainly produces goods for export.
We pride ourselves on producing high-quality products.
The local factory produces electronic components.

senses.text: to grow or make something as part of a natural process; to have a baby or young animal
examples: The region produces over 50 per cent of the country's wheat.
I would like to find out more about how our food is produced.
All our meat is locally produced.
These shrubs produce bright red berries.
Under stress, the body produces adrenalin.

senses.text: pleasant to look at, especially in a sexual way
examples: an attractive woman
I like John but I don't find him attractive physically.
I find men look more attractive as they age.

senses.text: pleasant
examples: a big house with an attractive garden
Your new glasses are very attractive.
Antique furniture is used to make an attractive contrast with a modern setting.
That's one of the less attractive aspects of her personality.
The city itself is very attractive.

senses.text: connected with or about love or a sexual relationship
examples: a romantic candlelit dinner
a romantic comedy
romantic stories/fiction
I'm not interested in a romantic relationship.
It wasn't even until a hundred or so years ago that the concept of romantic love in marriage gained any real popularity.

senses.text: showing feelings of love
examples: Why don't you ever give me flowers? I wish you'd be more romantic.
You're getting quite romantic in your old age!
I'm hopelessly romantic and dreamy.

senses.text: beautiful in a way that makes you think of love or feel strong emotions
examples: romantic music
romantic mountain scenery
romantic images of deserted beaches
It sounded romantic and exciting to work in the Walled City, but few stuck it more than a few weeks.

senses.text: not high or tall; not far above the ground
examples: a low wall/building/table
The fence is too low.
a low range of hills
low clouds
flying at low altitude

senses.text: at or near the bottom of something
examples: low back pain
the lower slopes of the mountain
temperatures in the low 20s (= no higher than 21–23°)

senses.text: below the usual or average amount, level or value
examples: a low level of unemployment
lower prices/costs
the lowest rates of interest for 40 years
the lowest temperature ever recorded
low-income families

senses.text: an invitation or a suggestion to somebody that they should enter a competition, fight, etc.
examples: She accepted his challenge to a debate on the issue.
I'm looking forward to taking on the challenge.
He plans to mount a challenge for the party leadership.

senses.text: a statement or an action that shows that somebody refuses to accept something and questions whether it is right, legal, etc.
examples: Their legal challenge was unsuccessful.
It was a direct challenge to the president's authority.
The commission reconsidered the rules after a challenge from the television networks.

senses.text: to cause a particular reaction or have a particular effect
examples: The announcement provoked a storm of protest.
The article was intended to provoke discussion.
Dairy products may provoke allergic reactions in some people.

senses.text: to say or do something that you know will annoy somebody so that they react in an angry way
examples: Be careful what you say—he's easily provoked.
The lawyer claimed his client was provoked into acts of violence by the defendant.
An attack on their city will only provoke them to retaliate.

senses.text: a large organized group of soldiers who are trained to fight on land
examples: The two opposing armies faced each other across the battlefield.
He commanded the army of occupation after the war.
Harold's exhausted army was/were defeated after nine hours of fighting.

senses.text: the part of a country’s armed forces that fights on land
examples: Her sister is in the army.
After leaving school, Mike went into the army.
He joined the army at the age of 16.
an army officer/chief
army barracks/bases

senses.text: the mass of salt water that covers most of the earth’s surface
examples: The ship was dredged from the depths of the ocean.
People were swimming in the ocean despite the hurricane warning.
The plane hit the ocean several miles offshore.
Our beach house is just a couple of miles from the ocean.
ships large enough to sail the open ocean

senses.text: one of the five large areas that the ocean is divided into
examples: the Antarctic/Arctic/Atlantic/Indian/Pacific Ocean
They travelled across oceans in search of a new life.

senses.text: a fine powder that consists of very small pieces of sand, earth, etc.
examples: A cloud of dust rose as the truck drove off.
Fine particles of dust will get everywhere.
The workers wear masks to avoid inhaling the dust.

senses.text: the fine powder of dirt that forms in buildings, on furniture, floors, etc.
examples: The books were all covered with dust.
There was a thick layer of dust on the table.
There wasn't a speck of dust anywhere in the room.
She is allergic to house dust.

senses.text: an arrangement with a company in which you pay them regular amounts of money and they agree to pay the costs, for example, if you die or are ill, or if you lose or damage something
examples: health/medical insurance
car/travel/unemployment insurance
Private insurance accounts for about 35 per cent of healthcare in the US.
to have/buy insurance
The bill would require employers to provide insurance for their workers.

senses.text: the business of providing people with insurance
examples: an insurance broker/company
the insurance industry/business
He works in insurance.
She's in insurance.

senses.text: to be used instead of somebody/something else; to do something instead of somebody/something else
examples: The new design will eventually replace all existing models.
Teachers will never be replaced by computers in the classroom.
These measures in no way replace the need for regular safety checks.
The weird feeling was gradually replaced by shock, and then anger.
She replaced Dr Hales as head teacher.

senses.text: to remove somebody/something and put another person or thing in their place
examples: He will be difficult to replace when he leaves.
proposed changes to the constitution to replace the current system of government
He was replaced as party leader in 1967.
It is not a good idea to miss meals and replace them with snacks.

senses.text: to change something that is old, damaged, etc. for a similar thing that is newer or better
examples: We're thinking of replacing our phone system.
to replace a window/roof
to replace a battery/light bulb
All the old carpets need replacing.
an operation to replace both hips

senses.text: to give somebody money for work, goods, services, etc.
examples: Who's paying?
I'll pay for the tickets.
You have to pay extra for a single room.
Many consumers are willing to pay more for better service.
Let me pay you for your time.

senses.text: to give somebody money that you owe them
examples: to pay a fee/bill/fine/debt
to pay your rent
Everyone has to pay their taxes.
The union paid all her legal costs.
Membership fees should be paid to the secretary.

senses.text: to work together with somebody else in order to achieve something
examples: The two groups agreed to cooperate with each other.
They had cooperated closely in the planning of the project.

senses.text: to be helpful by doing what somebody asks you to do
examples: Their captors told them they would be killed unless they cooperated.
The company has agreed to cooperate with the employment survey.

senses.text: an area in a yard where you grow flowers or plants
examples: These flowers brighten up backyard gardens all over the country.

senses.text: a public park
examples: Thousands of people now visit the gardens every year.
The concert will take place in the gardens.

senses.text: a statement of what may, must or must not be done in a particular situation or when playing a game
examples: She laid down strict rules for her tenants, including prompt payment of rent.
the rules of golf/tennis/football
to follow/obey a rule
to break/violate a rule
to enforce/apply a rule

senses.text: a statement of what is possible according to a particular system, for example the grammar of a language
examples: the basic rules of grammar
What is the rule for forming plurals?

senses.text: to send something from your hand through the air by moving your hand or arm quickly
examples: Practise throwing and catching.
They had a competition to see who could throw the furthest.
Who threw that rock?
Stop throwing stones at the window!
She threw the ball up and caught it again.

senses.text: to put something in a particular place quickly and carelessly
examples: Just throw your bag down over there.
He threw the keys casually down on the table.
A blanket was thrown over the bed.
I took some folders and started throwing stuff in them.

senses.text: something such as an object, a fact or a situation that shows, explains or supports what you say
examples: Let me give you an example.
It is important to cite examples to support your argument.
to provide/offer an example
I shall take four different examples from contemporary literature to illustrate my point.
Can you show me an example of what you mean?

senses.text: a thing that is typical of or represents a particular group or set
examples: This is a good example of the artist's early work.
It is a perfect example of this type of architecture.
Japan is often quoted as the prime example of a modern industrial nation.
It is a classic example of how not to design a new city centre.

senses.text: a male child or a young male person
examples: a little/small/young boy
I used to play here as a boy.
The older boys at school used to tease him.
Now she's a teenager, she's starting to be interested in boys.
Be a good boy and get me my coat.

senses.text: a young son
examples: They have two boys and a girl.
Her eldest boy is at college.

senses.text: to put or keep somebody/something in a place where they/it cannot be seen or found
examples: He hid the letter in a drawer.
I keep my private papers hidden.
They hid me from the police in their attic.

senses.text: to go somewhere where you hope you will not be seen or found
examples: Quick, hide!
I hid under the bed.
We hid from our pursuers in an empty house.
She hides herself away in her office all day.

senses.text: to cover somebody/something so that they/it cannot be seen
examples: He hid his face in his hands.
The house was hidden by trees.
The brim of his hat hid half his face.
No amount of make-up could hide the scar.

senses.text: to say or write the letters of a word in the correct order
examples: How do you spell your surname?
You've spelt my name wrong.
I thought her name was Catherine, but it's Kathryn spelt with a ‘K’.
Should 'internet' be spelled with a capital or lower case i?

senses.text: to form words correctly from individual letters
examples: I've never been able to spell.

senses.text: to form words when they are put together in a particular order
examples: C—A—T spells ‘cat’.

senses.text: the same thing or things
examples: I would do the same again.
I think the same as you do about this.
Just do the same as me (= as I do).
His latest movie is just more of the same—exotic locations, car chases and a final shoot-out.
‘I'll have coffee.’ ‘Same for me, please (= I will have one too).’

senses.text: having the same number, colour, size, quality, etc.
examples: There are several brands and they're not all the same.
I'd like one the same as yours.

senses.text: a way of communicating information, etc. to people
examples: the medium of radio/television
electronic/audiovisual media
The internet is the modern medium of communication.
A T-shirt can be an excellent medium for getting your message across.

senses.text: something that is used for a particular purpose
examples: English is the medium of instruction (= the language used to teach other subjects).
Video is a good medium for learning a foreign language.

senses.text: the act of causing somebody severe pain in order to punish them or make them say or do something
examples: Many of the refugees have suffered torture.
the use of torture
terrible instruments of torture
His confessions were made under torture.
I heard stories of gruesome tortures in prisons.

senses.text: mental or physical pain; something that causes this
examples: The interview was sheer torture from start to finish.

senses.text: to become or make somebody become calmer and less worried
examples: I'll only relax when I know you're safe.
I can't relax until I've spoken to her myself.
'You need to try and relax,' Jane said.
Relax! Everything will be OK.
A herbal tea will relax you.

senses.text: to become or make something become less tight or stiff
examples: Allow your muscles to relax completely.
The massage relaxed my tense back muscles.
He relaxed his grip on her arm.
He closed his eyes and forced himself to relax his tensed body.
The dictator refuses to relax his grip on power.

senses.text: to say something in a loud voice; to speak loudly/angrily to somebody
examples: Stop shouting and listen!
I shouted for help but nobody came.
Then he started shouting and swearing at her.
There are few things that get me shouting at the television.
We cannot learn until we stop shouting at each other.

senses.text: to make a loud noise
examples: She could hear him shouting and screaming in the next room.
I wanted to shout for joy.
He was hugging them and shouting with glee.
She shouted out in pain when she tried to move her leg.

senses.text: complete power to govern a country
examples: The country claimed sovereignty over the island.
This constitutes an attack on the sovereignty of Parliament.
the idea of consumer sovereignty

senses.text: the state of being a country with freedom to govern itself
examples: The declaration proclaimed the full sovereignty of the republic.

senses.text: people in general, living together in communities
examples: She believes that the arts benefit society as a whole.
Racism exists at all levels of society.
These children have grown up to be useful and productive members of society.
Every section of society must have access to education.
They carried out research into the roles of men and women in today’s society.

senses.text: a particular community of people who share the same customs, laws, etc.
examples: modern industrial societies
demand created by a consumer society
Can Britain ever be a classless society?
They were discussing the problems of Western society.
We live in a society that is obsessed with how people look.

senses.text: beautiful; attractive
examples: She looked particularly lovely that night.
He has a lovely voice.
You've got lovely eyes.
We travelled through some lovely countryside.
She's got an absolutely lovely face.

senses.text: very pleasant; wonderful
examples: ‘Can I get you anything?’ ‘A cup of tea would be lovely.’
What a lovely surprise!
How lovely to see you!
Isn't it a lovely day?
We've had a lovely time.

senses.text: very kind, generous and friendly
examples: Her mother was a lovely woman.
He is a lovely little boy.
She is a lovely old lady and always full of laughter.

senses.text: having a desire to cause physical or mental pain and make somebody suffer
examples: He was known to be a cruel dictator.
I can't stand people who are cruel to animals.
Her eyes were cruel and hard.
Sometimes you have to be cruel to be kind (= make somebody suffer because it will be good for them later).
an extremely cruel regime

senses.text: causing physical or mental pain and making somebody suffer
examples: a cruel joke/hoax
cruel punishment
It was a cruel irony that he, being gravely ill, would survive his family.
It would be a cruel twist of fate if he escaped only to starve to death once outside.
Her father's death was a cruel blow.

senses.text: the state of being similar to somebody/something but not exactly the same
examples: The report highlights the similarity between the two groups.
She bears a striking similarity to her mother.
There is some similarity in the way they sing.
In this work we can observe the stylistic similarity with Beethoven.
The results also showed the similarity of the two groups.

senses.text: a feature that things or people have that makes them like each other
examples: a study of the similarities and differences between the two countries
similarities in/of style
The karate bout has many similarities to a boxing match.

senses.text: an object, a piece of evidence or some information that helps the police solve a crime
examples: The burglar left no clues.
They are searching for clues about the kidnapping.
The police think the CCTV recording may offer clues to the identity of the killer.

senses.text: a fact or a piece of evidence that helps you discover the answer to a problem
examples: This data may provide clues about the current economic situation.
The trailer gives a few important clues about the film's plot.
Diet may hold the clue to the causes of migraine.
The text offers few clues as to his identity.

senses.text: some words or a piece of information that helps you find the answers to a crossword, a game or a question
examples: ‘You'll never guess who I saw today!’ ‘Give me a clue.’
a cryptic clue (= one that has a hidden meaning) from a crossword

senses.text: the rate at which something happens or is repeated
examples: Fatal road accidents have decreased in frequency over recent years.
Dawn was then visiting New York with increasing frequency.
a society with a high/low frequency (= happening often/not very often) of stable marriages
Users will be able to rearrange their icons by frequency of use.
The program can show us word frequency (= how often words occur in a language).

senses.text: the fact of something happening often
examples: the alarming frequency of computer errors
The relative frequency of this illness in the area is of concern to all doctors.
Objects like this turn up at sales with surprising frequency.
Bullets bounced off the rock with alarming frequency.

senses.text: the selling and transporting of goods to another country
examples: a ban on the export of live cattle
Then the fruit is packaged for export.
export earnings/subsidies
an export licence
Their main export market is the United States.

senses.text: a product that is sold to another country
examples: the country’s major exports
a fall in the value of exports
Oil exports have risen steadily.
The country's share of world exports of goods and services was 5.8 per cent.
Australia wants to increase its agricultural exports to Asia.

senses.text: a system that shows the level of prices and wages, etc. so that they can be compared with those of a previous date
examples: the cost-of-living index
The Dow Jones index fell 15 points this morning.
stock market indices
house price indexes

senses.text: a sign or measure that something else can be judged by
examples: The number of new houses being built is a good index of a country's prosperity.

senses.text: to make something or somebody ready to be used or to do something
examples: to prepare a report
A hotel room is being prepared for them.
The college prepares students for a career in business.
The training has prepared me to deal with any medical issue.
We all set about preparing for the party.

senses.text: to make yourself ready to do something or for something that you expect to happen
examples: I had no time to prepare.
The whole class is working hard preparing for the exams.
The police are preparing themselves for trouble at the demonstration.
I had been preparing myself for this moment.
Her condition is not improving as we hoped. You must prepare yourselves for the worst.

senses.text: to make food ready to be eaten
examples: He was in the kitchen preparing lunch.
to prepare meals/food/dinner

senses.text: an improvement in the condition or strength of something
examples: the revival of trade
an economic revival
a revival of property values

senses.text: the process of something becoming or being made popular or fashionable again
examples: a religious revival
Jazz is enjoying a revival.
a revival of interest in folk music

senses.text: a person who has the legal right to belong to a particular country
examples: The defeat of the president did not change the lives of ordinary citizens for the better.
an economic slowdown that has left millions of our fellow citizens unemployed
She's Italian by birth but is now an Australian citizen.
Polish citizens living in other parts of the European Union
the government's failure to address the concerns of the average citizen

senses.text: a person who lives in a particular place
examples: When you're old, people treat you like a second-class citizen.
There have been meetings of concerned citizens over proposed development plans.
the decent, law-abiding citizens of this city
the citizens of Budapest

senses.text: to admit, especially formally or to the police, that you have done something wrong or illegal
examples: After hours of questioning, the suspect confessed.
She confessed to the murder.
He confessed that he had stolen the money.
She confessed to me that she had known his true identity for some time.
We persuaded her to confess her crime.

senses.text: to admit something that you feel ashamed or embarrassed about
examples: She was reluctant to confess her ignorance.
I must confess to knowing nothing about golf.
I confess (that) I know nothing about golf.
‘I know nothing about it,’ he confessed.
I confess myself bewildered by their explanation.

senses.text: to keep a particular quality, feature, etc.; to make sure that something is kept
examples: He was anxious to preserve his reputation.
Efforts to preserve the peace have failed.
She managed to preserve her sense of humour under very trying circumstances.
He was insistent on preserving the integrity of the brand.

senses.text: to keep something in its original state in good condition
examples: a perfectly preserved 14th-century house
Is he really 60? He's remarkably well preserved.
We need knowledgeable gallery curators to preserve great masterpieces for posterity.
This vase has been preserved intact.

senses.text: to prevent something, especially food, from decaying (= being destroyed by natural processes) by treating it in a particular way
examples: Wax polish preserves wood and leather.
preserved lemons
olives preserved in brine
an attempt to preserve the corpse from decomposition

senses.text: feeling happy about something
examples: You're coming? I'm so pleased.
She was very pleased with her exam results.
Mary and I were most pleased with the present we bought in Edinburgh.
The boss should be pleased with you.
She seemed pleased at our success.

senses.text: happy or willing to do something
examples: We are always pleased to be able to help.
I was pleased to hear you've been promoted.
Aren't you pleased to see me?
Pleased to meet you (= said when you are introduced to somebody).
We are pleased to announce that the winner of our competition is…

senses.text: held or fixed in position securely; difficult to move, open or separate
examples: He kept a tight grip on her arm.
She twisted her hair into a tight knot.
The screw was so tight that it wouldn't move.
Dave's arms were tight around her waist.

senses.text: fitting closely to your body and sometimes uncomfortable
examples: She was wearing a tight pair of jeans.
These shoes are much too tight.
The new sweater was a tight fit.
The dress is a little tight across the chest.

senses.text: to admit that something is true, logical, etc. after first denying it or resisting it
examples: ‘Not bad,’ she conceded grudgingly.
He was forced to concede (that) there might be difficulties.
I had to concede the logic of this.
He reluctantly conceded the point to me.
He reluctantly conceded me the point.

senses.text: to admit that you have lost a game, an election, etc.
examples: After losing this decisive battle, the general was forced to concede.
Injury forced Hicks to concede defeat.

senses.text: to give something away, especially unwillingly; to allow somebody to have something
examples: The president was obliged to concede power to the army.
England conceded a goal immediately after half-time.
Women were only conceded full voting rights in the 1950s.
The firm should concede a significant salary increase to its employees.

senses.text: an agreement between countries, political parties, etc. to work together in order to achieve something that they all want
examples: to form/make an alliance
The Social Democrats are now in alliance with the Greens.
an alliance between education and business to develop the use of technology in schools

senses.text: a group of people, political parties, etc. who work together in order to achieve something that they all want
examples: The Green Alliance was formed to campaign against environmental damage.

senses.text: a series of letters, numbers, etc. that you must type into a computer or computer system in order to be able to use it
examples: Enter a username and password to get into the system.

senses.text: a secret word or phrase that you need to know in order to be allowed into a place
examples: You must give the password before they'll let you in.

senses.text: to formally suggest that somebody/something should be chosen for an important role, prize, position, etc.
examples: She has been nominated for the presidency.
He was nominated (as) best actor.
I nominated Paul to take on the role of treasurer.

senses.text: to choose somebody to do a particular job
examples: I have been nominated to the committee.
The president wanted to nominate her as his successor.
She was nominated to speak on our behalf.
Each office will nominate a representative to sit on the committee.

senses.text: a person whose job is presenting or talking on television or radio programmes
examples: She is a writer and broadcaster on environmental matters.

senses.text: a company that sends out television or radio programmes
examples: The country needs a national public service broadcaster like the BBC.

senses.text: the act of taking somebody/something away from a particular place
examples: Clearance of the site required the removal of a number of trees.
the removal of a tumour
Allegations of abuse led to the removal of several children from their families.

senses.text: the act of getting rid of something
examples: stain removal
the removal of trade barriers

senses.text: protected from any danger, harm or loss
examples: The children are quite safe here.
She didn't feel safe on her own.
Will the car be safe parked in the road?
They aimed to make the country safe from terrorist attacks.
Nobody is safe from suspicion at the moment.

senses.text: not likely to lead to any physical harm or danger
examples: a safe and effective remedy for coughs and colds
Is the water here safe to drink?
Acupuncture is safe for most people.
The street is not safe for children to play in.
It is one of the safest cars in the world.

senses.text: not harmed, damaged, lost, etc.
examples: We were glad she let us know she was safe.
The missing child was found safe and well.
They turned up safe and sound.
A reward was offered for the animal's safe return.
Take care of yourselves and stay safe.

senses.text: where somebody/something is not likely to be in danger or to be lost
examples: We all want to live in safer cities.
Keep your passport in a safe place.
We watched the explosion from a safe distance.
It was a relatively safe place compared with other countries.

senses.text: not involving much or any risk; not likely to be wrong or to upset somebody
examples: Electricity shares are still a safe investment.
As I peruse the menu the only safe bet is the grilled chicken.
The source of Harry's wealth was not a safe subject for public discussion.
It’s safe to assume (that) there will always be a demand for new software.
It's safe to say there aren't any movies out there quite like this one.

senses.text: an arrangement that you make, with a shop for example, to pay later for something you buy
examples: to get credit
We bought the dishwasher on credit.
to offer interest-free credit (= allow somebody to pay later, without any extra charge)
Someone with a bad credit history is less likely to be lent money.
a credit agreement

senses.text: money that you borrow from a bank; a loan
examples: The bank refused further credit to the company.
Local lenders are more likely to extend credit (= lend money) to smaller, more marginal borrowers.

senses.text: to take liquid, air, etc. into your mouth by using the muscles of your lips
examples: to suck the juice from an orange
She was noisily sucking up milk through a straw.
He sucked the blood from a cut on his finger.

senses.text: to keep something in your mouth and pull on it with your lips and tongue
examples: The baby sucked at its mother's breast.
He sucked at the wound on his hand.
She sucked on a mint.
She sucked a mint.
Stop sucking your thumb!

senses.text: to take liquid, air, etc. out of something
examples: The pump sucks air out through the valve.
Greenfly can literally suck a plant dry.
The machine sucks up mud and stones from the bottom of the pond.

senses.text: to pull somebody/something with great force in a particular direction
examples: The canoe was sucked down into the whirlpool.
The mud had sucked him in up to his waist.

senses.text: a person or thing that is not included in a general statement
examples: Most of the buildings in the town are modern, but the church is an exception.
With very few exceptions, private schools get the best exam results.
Nobody had much money at the time and I was no exception.

senses.text: a thing that does not follow a rule
examples: Good writing is unfortunately the exception rather than the rule (= it is unusual).
There are always a lot of exceptions to grammar rules.

senses.text: a track with rails on which trains run
examples: The railway is still under construction.
a disused railway
They are doing maintenance work on the railway.
Follow the path west along the old railway track.
a railway bridge/tunnel

senses.text: a system of tracks, together with the trains that run on them, and the organization and people needed to operate them
examples: Her father worked on the railways.
a railway station
a railway worker/company
East Midlands Railway
a model railway

senses.text: to admit that you have been defeated and want to stop fighting; to allow yourself to be caught, taken prisoner, etc.
examples: The rebel soldiers were forced to surrender.
He surrendered voluntarily to his enemies.
The hijackers eventually surrendered themselves to the police.

senses.text: to give up something/somebody when you are forced to
examples: He agreed to surrender all claims to the property.
They surrendered their guns to the police.
The defendant was released to await trial but had to surrender her passport.
The dictator surrendered power to Parliament.

senses.text: the act of printing a book, a magazine, etc. and making it available to the public; a book, a magazine, etc. that has been published
examples: specialist publications
the publication date
The study is published this week as an advance online publication.
He has written for various publications.
the publication of his first novel

senses.text: the act of printing something in a newspaper, report, etc. so that the public knows about it
examples: The Home Secretary attempted to get an injunction to prevent publication, but was unsuccessful.
a delay in the publication of the exam results
The newspaper continues to defend its publication of the photographs.

senses.text: good things that happen to you by chance, not because of your own efforts or abilities
examples: With any luck, we'll be home before dark.
With a bit of luck, we'll finish on time.
So far I have had no luck with finding a job.
I'm not having much luck with buses.
I never had much luck (= had a successful relationship) with women.

senses.text: chance; the force that causes good or bad things to happen to people
examples: to have good/bad luck
I put the loss of the money down to pure bad luck.
We had horrible luck with the weather.
In many cultures the colour blue is believed to bring good luck.
myths based on the idea that owls bring bad luck

senses.text: a university course that is of a higher level than a basic course (in the US also used to describe a class in school that is at a higher level than other classes)
examples: an honours degree/course
a First Class Honours degree
I took an honors class in English.

senses.text: if you pass an exam or graduate from a university or school with honours, you receive a special grade for having achieved a very high standard
examples: He passed with second-class honours.

senses.text: causing or able to cause death
examples: She had been given a lethal dose of poison.
Any sharp pointed instrument is potentially a lethal weapon.
The closure of the factory dealt a lethal blow to the town.
The snake’s venom is rarely lethal to humans.

senses.text: causing or able to cause a lot of harm or damage
examples: You and that car—it's a lethal combination!
A shift in shopping habits created a lethal cocktail of bankruptcies and store closures.
